text.title: RICE Scoring Process: Six Steps, Sanity Check, and Strategic Veto
text.summary: The six-step RICE scoring process, the sanity check, the strategic veto rule, and the quarterly re-scoring cadence.

rule.statement: Gather all candidate features with a one-paragraph problem statement each before scoring begins. Score the problem statement, not the feature title.
rule.rationale: Scoring vague feature titles produces vague scores. The problem statement forces the scorer to articulate who is affected and why, grounding the R and I estimates.

rule.statement: Score in batches of 8-10 items maximum. Quality drops sharply after 10. Randomize order within each batch and average two independent passes to prevent anchoring on the first item.
rule.rationale: LLMs anchor on the first item scored and bias subsequent scores relative to it. Randomization across passes neutralizes this effect.

rule.statement: Store raw R/I/C/E inputs and rationale alongside the final score. The score alone is unauditable; rationale enables future calibration.
rule.rationale: Without stored rationale, post-ship actuals cannot be compared against estimates, making quarterly calibration impossible.

section.title: Step 3: Calculate RICE

rule.statement: Apply the formula: (R × I × C) / E. Treat confidence as a decimal (80% = 0.8). Sort highest to lowest.
rule.rationale: Treating confidence as a percentage (80) instead of a decimal (0.8) produces scores 100x larger and loses the relative weighting between confidence levels.

section.reference: Bash script that reads a CSV and outputs sorted RICE scores with the computed column.

rule.statement: After sorting, ask: "Does this ranking feel wrong?" Investigate items where the gut diverges from the score — the score is probably right and the gut is anchored on a wrong assumption. Document the investigation.
rule.rationale: Sanity checks catch data errors (wrong Reach unit, Effort in days instead of months). Documenting the investigation builds calibration discipline.

rule.statement: After sorting, the PM may promote one strategic bet that ranks lower than its score suggests. Document the veto rationale explicitly. Strategic veto is limited to one item per quarter.
rule.rationale: RICE does not encode dependencies, sequencing, or strategic narrative. A single veto slot preserves the score discipline while allowing human judgment on moat-building investments.

rule.statement: Re-score the full backlog quarterly. Re-score individual features immediately when their underlying assumption is invalidated — not at the next quarterly cycle. Track post-ship actuals (real Reach, real Effort) to calibrate next quarter's estimates.
rule.rationale: A score based on stale assumptions misleads roadmap planning. Immediate re-scoring on assumption change prevents cascading prioritization errors.

antipattern.description: Re-scoring retroactively to "explain" why a shipped feature succeeded.
antipattern.reason: Retroactive scoring destroys the calibration data needed to improve future estimates. Keep original scores immutable; add actuals as a separate column.
